north_india.name: Dashashwamedh Ghat
north_india.description: Most spectacular ghat in Varanasi, famous for the evening Ganga Aarti ceremony attended by thousands daily. Named after the ten-horse sacrifice performed by Lord Brahma. The ghat buzzes with activity from dawn to midnight with priests, pilgrims, and tourists.
north_india.significance: Main ghat for Ganga Aarti, witness to daily spiritual ceremonies, cremation site beliefs
north_india.best_time_to_visit: October to March (winter), Evening for Ganga Aarti (6:30 PM)
north_india.timings: Open 24 hours, Aarti at 6:30 PM daily
north_india.architecture: Stone steps leading to river, multiple shrines
north_india.food_options: Street food, lassi shops, local restaurants
north_india.safety_tips: Watch for pickpockets, avoid aggressive boat operators, respect cremation sites
north_india.avoid_common_mistake: Don't photograph cremation ceremonies at Manikarnika - highly disrespectful

north_india.name: Manikarnika Ghat
north_india.description: The most sacred cremation ghat in Varanasi where cremations happen 24/7. According to Hindu belief, dying and being cremated here grants moksha (liberation from cycle of rebirth). The ghat is named after Goddess Parvati's earring (manikarnika) that fell here.
north_india.significance: Primary cremation site, believed to grant moksha, eternal sacred fire burning continuously for 3,500+ years
north_india.best_time_to_visit: October to March, early morning for fewer crowds
north_india.safety_tips: Stay respectful, don't take photos, avoid aggressive guides demanding money, don't disturb mourning families
north_india.avoid_common_mistake: NEVER photograph cremations - extremely offensive. Don't ask intrusive questions to mourning families. Fake 'priests' may demand money - decline politely.

north_india.name: Assi Ghat
north_india.description: Southernmost major ghat in Varanasi where River Assi meets the Ganges. Popular with students and foreigners, known for peaceful atmosphere, yoga sessions, and cultural programs. Less crowded than central ghats.
north_india.significance: Site where Sant Tulsidas lived, confluence of Assi and Ganga, evening Aarti
north_india.best_time_to_visit: October to March, morning for yoga, evening for aarti
north_india.avoid_common_mistake: Don't expect intense spiritual experience like Dashashwamedh - this is more relaxed and tourist-friendly

north_india.name: Harishchandra Ghat
north_india.description: Second main cremation ghat in Varanasi, named after the truthful King Harishchandra who worked here as a cremation ground keeper. Less crowded than Manikarnika but equally sacred. Ancient texts describe this as one of the oldest ghats in Varanasi.
north_india.significance: Ancient cremation site, legend of King Harishchandra, moksha belief, associated with truth and sacrifice
north_india.safety_tips: Same as Manikarnika - maintain respectful distance, no photography, avoid fake guides
north_india.avoid_common_mistake: Same rules as Manikarnika - no photography, maintain respectful distance. Don't disturb mourning families.

north_india.name: Panchganga Ghat
north_india.description: Believed to be confluence of five sacred rivers: Ganga, Yamuna, Saraswati, Kirana, and Dhupapapa. Features impressive Mughal-era Alamgir Mosque above the ghat. The name literally means 'five rivers' in Hindi. The ghat showcases Hindu-Muslim architectural harmony.
north_india.significance: Five rivers confluence (mythological), important bathing ghat, architectural heritage site
north_india.best_time_to_visit: October to March, early morning for holy bath
north_india.architecture: Wide stone steps, Alamgir Mosque (built by Aurangzeb), ancient Bindu Madhav Temple site
north_india.avoid_common_mistake: The five rivers confluence is mythological - you won't see five separate rivers. Don't ask locals to point them out.

north_india.name: Kedar Ghat
north_india.description: Home to the ancient Kedareshwar Temple dedicated to Lord Shiva, believed to be one of the twelve Jyotirlingas. The ghat is spiritually significant and attracts devotees year-round. The temple's lingam is said to have a southward tilt, unique among Shiva temples.
north_india.significance: Houses Kedareshwar Temple, associated with Kedarnath pilgrimage, sacred Shiva site
north_india.best_time_to_visit: October to March, Monday mornings (auspicious for Shiva worship)
north_india.timings: Ghat open 24 hours, Temple 5 AM - 12 PM, 4 PM - 9 PM
north_india.dress_code: Modest clothing, remove shoes before entering temple
north_india.photography: Allowed at ghat, restricted inside temple
north_india.architecture: Stone steps, ancient temple architecture, multiple smaller shrines
things_to_do: Take holy dip before temple visit
north_india.safety_tips: Temple gets crowded during Shravan month, visit early morning to avoid crowds
north_india.avoid_common_mistake: Don't confuse this with Kedarnath in Uttarakhand - this is a separate sacred site. Temple priests may ask for donations - contribute as you wish, no compulsion.

north_india.name: Man Mandir Ghat
north_india.description: Built in 1600 by Raja Man Singh of Amber (Jaipur), this ghat is famous for its stunning architecture and the Jantar Mantar observatory built by Sawai Jai Singh II in 1710. The observatory is one of five astronomical observatories built across India and represents advanced scientific knowledge of the era.
north_india.significance: Historical observatory, architectural marvel, scientific heritage site, royal patronage
north_india.best_time_to_visit: October to March, daytime for observatory visit
north_india.timings: Ghat open 24 hours, Observatory 6 AM - 6 PM
north_india.architecture: Rajasthani style palace, stone astronomical instruments, ornate balconies and windows, carved pillars
north_india.accommodation_nearby: Heritage hotels in nearby buildings, guesthouses
things_to_do: Take boat ride for best ghat view
north_india.safety_tips: Observatory stairs can be steep, wear comfortable shoes. Best viewed from boats for complete architecture appreciation.
north_india.avoid_common_mistake: Many tourists skip the observatory thinking it's just a ghat - the astronomical instruments are the main attraction. Hire a local guide to understand the scientific instruments.
north_india.historical_note: The observatory instruments include sundials and angular measurement devices used for calculating planetary positions

north_india.name: Tulsi Ghat
north_india.description: Named after the great Hindu poet-saint Goswami Tulsidas, who wrote the Ramcharitmanas (Hindi version of Ramayana) here in the 16th century. The ghat is considered sacred by devotees of Lord Rama. A small house where Tulsidas lived and wrote still stands near the ghat.
north_india.significance: Birthplace of Ramcharitmanas, associated with Saint Tulsidas, important for Rama devotees
north_india.best_time_to_visit: October to March, Ram Navami festival (March-April)
north_india.architecture: Simple stone steps, Tulsidas' house (museum), small Rama temple
north_india.local_transport: Auto-rickshaw, walking distance from other central ghats
things_to_do: Visit during Ram Navami for grand celebrations
north_india.safety_tips: Quieter than main ghats, safe for solo travelers and meditation
north_india.avoid_common_mistake: The ghat is less commercialized - don't expect elaborate aartis like Dashashwamedh. The spiritual significance here is literary and devotional rather than ritualistic.
north_india.crowd_level: Low to Moderate (High during Ram Navami)
north_india.literary_significance: Tulsidas composed Ramcharitmanas here, making it a pilgrimage site for Hindi literature lovers
